sn47.10:2.1: The sense of pubbenāpara as “than they had before” (literally, “after than before”) is established at DN 26:9.6, where subjects do not prosper as they had before under a righteous king.
sn47.10:6.2: The Buddha was speaking of “monks” (bhikkhu) and “nuns” (bhikkhunī) and now returns to the default masculine form bhikkhu, which is inclusive of both monks and nuns, and thus rendered with the literal and gender-neutral “mendicant”.
sn47.10:6.3: Pariḷāha (“sweltering”) is used in the sense of a “fever” which, arising from mental or physical causes, causes distress in the body (AN 3.35:4.2). Elsewhere it’s used for exceptionally hot and humid weather (Bu Pc 57:7.1.14). It is not uncommon for a meditator to experience symptoms such as sweating when the meditation is not yet in balance.
sn47.10:6.4: An “inspiring” (pasādanīya) meditation subject (nimitta) is one that promotes joy in that meditator. This is very personal, but for different meditators it might be love, or the breath, or the recollection of the qualities of the Buddha.
sn47.10:6.10: “Reflecting” (paṭisañcikkhati) is in reference to the reviewing (commentary: paccavekkhati) that a good meditator always does at the end of the practice.
sn47.10:6.11: The mind has been “directed” to a certain meditation subject for the purpose of attaining samādhi. This is in reference to vitakka and vicāra, which have a decisive function in attaining and maintaining the first absorption.
sn47.10:6.12: To paṭisaṁharati is to “desist” from a bad deed (MN 61:10.6), to “unwrap” smothering coils (Ud 2.1:3.2), to “pull back” the hand (MN 48:11.7), or to “relax” the bow (DN 3:1.23.9). Here the meditator, confident in their attainment so far, knows that further progress depends not on intensifying effort, but on easing back.
sn47.10:6.14: This phrase indicates second and higher absorptions, as freedom from vitakka and vicāra, and inner contentment are characteristics of the second absorption, while mindful bliss is characteristic of the third.
sn47.10:8.2: The specification of not directing the mind “externally” only is curious, since mindfulness meditation is normally practiced both internally and externally. The commentary explains that the meditator does not abandon their original meditation object to focus on some other external object, in contrast with the “directed” method. The idea seems to be that a more advanced meditator, since they no longer experience symptoms such as sweltering body, need not employ different strategies to overcome obstacles, but can simply proceed with the meditation and allow the outcomes to flow naturally.
